attributes.stem:
A 67-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of increasing right upper quadrant pain, jaundice and rigors. He has a medical history of ischaemic heart disease and hypertension. His regular medications are aspirin, perindopril and rosuvastatin.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 39.4°C. There is tenderness on deep palpation in the right upper quadrant of his abdomen.

Investigations reveal: white cell count 18 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0), bilirubin 93 μmol/L (3-20), ALT 464 U/L (5-40), AST 129 U/L (5-40), GGT 1659 U/L (5-50), ALP 206 U/L (30-110). Renal function and electrolytes are normal. An ultrasound demonstrates stones in the gallbladder and a dilated common bile duct. Intravenous fluids and antibiotics are commenced. An ERCP is arranged.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the primary purpose of the ERCP in this patient?
options: To extract any biliary tract calculi
To drain the common bile duct
To define the biliary tract anatomy
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of cholangitis: right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, and fever. The significantly elevated liver enzymes, particularly the cholestatic pattern (markedly raised GGT and ALP), along with hyperbilirubinaemia and leukocytosis, support the diagnosis, as do findings of biliary obstruction on ultrasound. This is most likely secondary to gallstones obstructing the common bile duct. It must be remembered that ultrasonography is not absolute and will miss some 30% of cases of duct stones. The stasis of bile flow, as evident by the significantly raised GGT + ALP, promotes a foci of bacterial colonisation and subsequent infection.

In this scenario, the patient is septic, evidenced by tachycardia, hypotension, and fever. The source of sepsis is almost certainly an infected biliary system containing pus. Acute cholangitis is a medical emergency that requires prompt management to prevent progression to severe sepsis and multi-organ failure.

The primary purpose of ERCP in this case is to drain the common bile duct. This is crucial for two reasons:

1. It achieves source control of the infection by allowing the infected bile and pus to drain.

2. It relieves the biliary obstruction, restoring normal bile flow and reducing inflammation in the liver.

All of the options listed are potential roles of ERCP, however, in this stem it is unlikely to be an underlying malignancy and the biliary tract anatomy is not the most critical component. Whilst in this case it would be hoped that a stent could be inserted to facilitate ongoing drainage, this is not the primary objective. If, for whatever reason a stent cannot be inserted, then the sphincterotomy alone will facilitate biliary drainage.

To extract any biliary tract calculi is also important and should be attempted during the ERCP if possible. However, even if stone extraction is not immediately successful, drainage alone can significantly improve the patient's condition.

To exclude any underlying malignancy is not the primary concern in this acute setting. While ERCP can provide diagnostic information, the immediate priority is therapeutic intervention.

To define the biliary tract anatomy is also not the primary purpose in this emergency situation, although understanding the anatomy is necessary to perform the procedure effectively.

In summary, while ERCP can serve multiple purposes, in acute cholangitis, the primary and most urgent goal is to drain the obstructed and infected biliary system. This, along with appropriate antibiotic therapy, forms the cornerstone of managing acute cholangitis.
learningPoints: Acute cholangitis is a medical emergency requiring early IV antibiotics and biliary drainage.
The primary purpose of ERCP in acute cholangitis is to drain the common bile duct and relieve biliary obstruction.
references: Therapeutic Guidelines. Acute Cholangitis. Victoria: eTG; 2019 [cited 2020 december 23]. Available from: https://tgldcdp-tg-org-au.proxy.library.adelaide.edu.au/viewTopic?topicfile=acute-cholangitis&guidelineName=Antibiotic&topicNavigation=navigateTopic#toc_d1e47

Cozma MA, Găman MA, Srichawla BS, Dhali A, Manan MR, Nahian A, Marsool MD, Suteja RC, Kutikuppala LV, Kipkorir V, Găman AM. Acute cholangitis: a state-of-the-art review. Annals of Medicine and Surgery. 2024 Aug 1;86(8):4560-74
